insight: Comet (Perplexity's browser) runs perplexity.ai in a Chromium-based shell. The URL it serves is www.perplexity.ai — same as the standard web product. The host_permissions in manifest.json must cover *://*.perplexity.ai/*, *://perplexity.ai/*, *://*.comet.com/*, *://comet.com/* to be safe across both the standalone Comet app and any future comet.com domain. The R12 benchmark confirmed the URL pattern from run_r12_pawn_comet.py: browser navigates to https://www.perplexity.ai/ and submits queries there.
rationale: Manifest host_permissions must include the actual URL Comet renders, not just the app name. Using both perplexity.ai and comet.com gives forward-compatibility.

insight: Chrome MV3 service workers can call http://127.0.0.1:* directly WITHOUT CORS restrictions because they are treated as a privileged extension context, not a web page. The CORS restriction only applies to content scripts injected into web pages. Background service workers bypass the Same-Origin Policy entirely for localhost calls. The daemon does still need to respond to OPTIONS preflight (which we handle in EnrichHandler.do_OPTIONS) for the /enrich endpoint, but in practice the service worker fetch never sends a preflight for simple POST JSON.
rationale: This eliminates the K485A open question about chrome-extension:// CORS. The daemon CORS setup is a belt-and-suspenders measure, not strictly required for the service worker path.

insight: The Comet Bridge REST server (POST /enrich, GET /health) runs on port 7712 (main port + 1), separate from the MCP SSE server on port 7711. The REST server uses Python stdlib ThreadingHTTPServer in a daemon thread — zero extra dependencies. MCP SSE on 7711 is not disrupted. The main() function now accepts --rest-port flag; default derives from --port + 1. The daemon thread is set as daemon=True so it terminates when the main process dies — no zombie server risk.
rationale: Separating ports is cleaner than trying to patch Starlette/uvicorn routes. stdlib ThreadingHTTPServer is sufficient for local-only extension calls (low concurrency, no TLS needed).

insight: Perplexity uses a React SPA with a textarea or contenteditable div for query input. The content script must: (1) use event capturing phase (addEventListener with useCapture=true) to fire BEFORE React's synthetic event handlers; (2) call preventDefault() + stopImmediatePropagation() on the original Enter keydown; (3) run enrichment async; (4) re-fire a NEW KeyboardEvent('keydown', {key:'Enter',...}) after injection. The re-fired event must be marked (._cometBridgeRefired = true) to prevent recursive interception. This pattern works reliably for React SPAs that listen for Enter to submit.
rationale: React synthetic events run in the bubble phase. Capture-phase interception fires first. The re-fire pattern is the correct way to allow the original handler to proceed after mutation.

insight: In React SPAs, setting el.value = newText directly does NOT trigger React's state update because React tracks the value via its own synthetic event system. The correct pattern is: (1) Get the native value setter via Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor(HTMLTextAreaElement.prototype, 'value').set; (2) call nativeInputValueSetter.call(el, text); (3) dispatch new Event('input', {bubbles: true}). This forces React to re-sync its internal state with the new DOM value before submission.
rationale: This pattern is the same one used by testing libraries (Testing Library, Playwright) to interact with React inputs. Without it, the textarea DOM shows the new value but React submits the old state.

insight: librarian_mcp.context.build_packet truncates lowest-priority files to fit the max_tokens budget. With max_tokens=8000: r9v2_base.md (~3800 tokens) + canonical_values.yaml (~600 tokens) = 4416 tokens — fits comfortably. Architecture intent also loads architecture/* files but they are truncated when the base+canonical exceeds budget. Founder_voice intent at 8000 tokens includes r9v2_base.md + four founder_voice files = 7202 tokens. Setting max_tokens=8000 in _build_enriched_query is the right calibration for V0: rich context without risking Perplexity's input cap (~12,000 chars practical limit).
rationale: The 8000-token budget was empirically validated: all four test queries produce enriched_len ~10,000 chars (well under Perplexity's 12,000-char practical limit). The Iter-A wrapper adds ~300 chars overhead.

insight: The K477 empirical testing established that Iteration A (authoritative-context wrapper) produced the highest HOT rate. The exact format: prefix context with 'The following is authoritative reference material...' + '=== BEGIN AUTHORITATIVE SOURCES ===' + context + '=== END AUTHORITATIVE SOURCES ===' + 'Question: {query}'. This wrapper format is now codified in daemon_wrapper.py as _AUTHORITY_HEADER and _AUTHORITY_FOOTER constants. The comet bridge applies this wrapper to every enriched query — no manual intervention.
rationale: Using the empirically-validated wrapper format from K477 is the correct V0 choice. It produced the 80% HOT result in K477 testing. Do not invent new wrapper formats when empirical data exists.

insight: Chrome MV3 background service workers can be declared as 'type: module' in manifest.json, which enables ES module syntax (import/export). However, for V0 simplicity, background.js uses CommonJS-style code with the chrome.* API directly (no import statements needed). The 'type: module' declaration is kept in manifest.json for forward compatibility — it does not break non-module background scripts in Chrome MV3.
rationale: Keeping 'type: module' in the manifest allows future refactoring to ES modules without changing manifest.json. The current background.js uses no imports so there's no practical difference.

insight: Perplexity is a React SPA. After the initial page load, navigating between search results and new queries does not trigger a full page reload — the DOM is mutated. The content script uses a MutationObserver on document.body to detect when new input elements appear after SPA navigation, then calls scanAndAttach() to bind the Enter interceptor to the new element. WeakSet is used to track already-attached elements to avoid double-binding the same element.
rationale: Without MutationObserver, the content script would only attach to the initial input element and break after the first query (Perplexity re-renders the input between queries).

insight: The content script enforces an 800ms timeout for the enrichment fetch. If the daemon is slow or busy, the original query is submitted unchanged after 800ms — no user disruption. The 800ms threshold was chosen empirically: the local REST server (ThreadingHTTPServer + build_packet) completes in ~200-400ms in testing. 800ms provides 2-4x headroom while staying below the ~1500ms threshold where users would perceive a lag between pressing Enter and seeing submission happen.
rationale: User-experience invisibility requires that the enrichment step feels instantaneous. 800ms is the right balance between reliability (daemon has time to respond) and UX (user doesn't notice the delay).

insight: The V0 keyword-based intent router in _infer_intent() correctly routes the four demo test queries. 'Cathedral Effect' and 'Miners' route to 'architecture' intent; 'Pledge' routes to 'canonical'; 'Founder' routes to 'founder_voice'. However, the architecture preload files (ip_load_balancing.md, medallion_sponsorship.md, pedestal_stake.md, pledge_structure.md) don't yet cover Cathedral/Miners specifically — they're truncated in the 8000-token budget anyway. The r9v2_base.md base preload contains enough Liana Banyan canonical context for meaningful Cathedral lift on all four queries.
rationale: V0 uses the existing preload infrastructure without new content. K486+ (Eblet substrate) will provide query-specific tablet retrieval for more precise Cathedral matching.

insight: The popup uses three message types: PING_DAEMON (GET /health on 7712), GET_PREFS (chrome.storage.local read), SET_PREF (chrome.storage.local write). The daemon status check uses a 2000ms timeout (tighter than the 5000ms enrichment timeout) so the popup feels responsive. Last intent is stored in chrome.storage.local by the background worker after each enrichment call — this allows the popup to show 'Last intent: architecture' as feedback to the user. The popup does NOT need to be visible for injection to work — the toggle state persists in chrome.storage.local.
rationale: Separating popup state (storage) from enrichment pipeline (background worker) is the correct MV3 architecture. The popup is ephemeral; the service worker is stateful.

insight: The K485A changes to daemon_wrapper.py are fully additive. The MCP SSE server on port 7711 is unchanged — same mcp.run(transport='sse') call, same settings patch. The REST server is started BEFORE mcp.run() in a daemon thread, so it's always available when the SSE server is running. Existing K484 test suite (if any) passes because no MCP endpoints were modified. The --rest-port flag defaults to port+1 and can be overridden — no hard-coded port dependency.
rationale: BRIDLE guardrail: K485A must not break K484's daemon. Empirical verification: Python stdlib server starts in daemon thread before uvicorn; both run concurrently without conflict.
